title: A Mathematical Theory of Quantum Sheaf Cohomology
speaker: Ron Donagi

proof_stages.label: Physical target and omalous bundle condition
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates quantum sheaf cohomology from the (0,2) A/2 model: for an omalous holomorphic bundle E with c_1(E)=c_1(TX) and c_2(E)=c_2(TX), the classical sheaf-cohomology or “polymology” ring is expected to acquire quantum corrections.

proof_stages.label: Restriction to toric GLSM data and deformed Euler sequence
proof_stages.summary: Since stable-map correlation functions are not mathematically available in this setting, the speaker restricts to smooth projective toric varieties and deformations of the tangent bundle encoded by homogeneous toric data and a deformed toric Euler sequence.

proof_stages.label: Genus-zero GLSM compactifications and induced bundles
proof_stages.summary: For each curve class beta, the genus-zero GLSM compactification is obtained by compactifying polynomial map data, producing a toric space X_beta together with a natural deformation E_beta of its tangent bundle.

proof_stages.label: Classical polymology via Koszul complexes and primitive collections
proof_stages.summary: Replacing E^* by the two-term complex Z -> W and taking exterior powers gives Koszul sequences whose toric line-bundle cohomology detects primitive collections and yields explicit classical relations Q_K, recovering the Stanley-Reisner ideal when E=TX and showing that nonlinear deformation terms drop out.

proof_stages.label: Beta-sector quantum relations and obstruction insertions
proof_stages.summary: The same primitive-collection calculation on X_beta repeats rays with multiplicities h^0, giving beta-sector relations built from powers of the same determinants Q_C, while h^1 multiplicities supply the four-Fermi obstruction factor f_beta correcting excess dimension.

proof_stages.label: Normalization across sectors and final quantum relations
proof_stages.summary: Multiplication by suitable powers of the Q_C gives compatible transition maps between the one-dimensional top targets for different beta, and the direct-limit normalization leads to the global quantum sheaf cohomology relations indexed by primitive collections.

examples.name: Deformations of T_X plus trivial summands
examples.note: The speaker mentions deformations of T_X\oplus O^k as easy omalous bundles, illustrating the Chern-class anomaly-cancellation condition without being used in the computation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites deformations of T_X plus trivial O-summands as an obvious homologous-bundle source with the right Chern classes, but does not compute or run the theory on that case

passes.reason: A deformation of T_X with added trivial summands is used as a standard source of omalous bundles, not to refute anything.
a stabilized tangent-bundle construction with trivial summands is used as a positive source of omalous bundles, not as a refutation.

A systematic family of omalous, stably tangent bundles is used as a positive source of examples, not as an obstruction.

It is a stabilized tangent bundle construction with Chern-trivial O-summands, used as a standard omalous family.

An infinite standard family of omalous bundles used as a positive source of examples, not to refute anything.
Standard stabilized deformations of the tangent bundle used as positive omalous examples, not to refute a claim.
A systematic stabilized deformation family used as an obvious source of omalous bundles, not as a failure case or sporadic/extreme object.
used as an omalous-bundle construction, with the added O-summands making it a stabilized partly trivial bundle rather than an obstruction
A systematic family of omalous bundles obtained from the tangent bundle and trivial summands, used constructively rather than as an obstruction.

examples.name: P^1 x P^1 running example
examples.note: The P^1 x P^1 calculation is presented as the example whose unwieldy earlier computations led the authors to the Koszul/homological-algebra explanation and then to primitive collections in general.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses P^1 x P^1 throughout, writing its fan, divisors, coordinate ring, Picard generators, map compactifications, wedge decompositions, cohomology vanishings, and explicit q and q-tilde relations

passes.reason: P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous toric surface used as the running model example.
P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous toric surface with square fan symmetry, used as a running computation.
P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous product used as the running computation, not to disprove a claim.

P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous/toric surface used as the running illustrative case.

A highly regular toric product surface used as the running calculation example, not adversarial
The homogeneous toric surface P^1 x P^1 is used as a running computational model rather than an obstruction.

The highly regular homogeneous toric surface P^1 x P^1 is used as a running illustration of the computation.
used as the running toric calculation, and P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous product with square-fan symmetry
The product P^1 x P^1 is a highly regular homogeneous toric surface with a square fan, used as the running model example.

examples.name: Euler sequence on projective space
examples.note: The usual projective-space Euler sequence is recalled only to make the toric Euler sequence for the cotangent bundle recognizable.
examples.engagement_reason: the Euler sequence on projective space is recalled as the projective-space instance of the toric cotangent sequence, without further calculation on projective space itself

passes.reason: The Euler sequence on projective space is a canonical construction on a maximally symmetric variety, used as the familiar special case.
the Euler sequence is the canonical homogeneous projective-space resolution used to motivate its toric analogue.
The Euler sequence is the canonical PGL-equivariant sequence on maximally homogeneous projective space, used as a model case.

The Euler sequence is the canonical homogeneous sequence on maximally symmetric projective space, used as the prototype for the toric sequence.
The Euler sequence on P^n is the standard PGL-equivariant exact sequence on maximally symmetric projective space.

used as the standard model for the toric cotangent sequence, and the Euler sequence on projective space is canonical and PGL-equivariant
The Euler sequence is the canonical equivariant exact sequence on maximally symmetric projective space, used as the standard case motivating the toric generalization.

examples.name: Ordinary quantum cohomology of projective space
examples.note: The relation x^{n+1}=q in QH^*(P^n) is used as a familiar analogy for replacing classical primitive-collection relations by quantum ones.
examples.engagement_reason: ordinary quantum cohomology of projective space is invoked only as a familiar analogy, with the standard relation x^{n+1}=q stated rather than derived

passes.reason: Quantum cohomology of projective space reflects the symmetry of projective space and is invoked only as an analogy for the new relations.
ordinary QH*(P^n) is the standard projective-space quantum ring used as an analogy for the new relations.
